worth_it_explanation: Frequency and exclusion settings in The Trade Desk control how often a user sees the campaign and which users should never see it at all. A frequency cap applied only at the ad group level allows a user who qualifies for several ad groups in the same campaign to receive an ad from each of them, so the campaign-level exposure runs well above the intended cap. A suppression list that has not been refreshed continues serving to recent converters, paying again for users who already responded. These checks read directly from the campaign structure and the Audiences section in the Kokai platform and typically resolve through configuration changes rather than rebuilds.
intro: The Trade Desk's Kokai platform applies frequency caps at the campaign level so a single cap governs how many times a user sees an ad across all ad groups in that campaign. Without a campaign-level cap, each ad group applies its own limit independently, and a user who qualifies for multiple ad groups can receive the full per-ad-group allowance from each one. Alongside frequency, suppression and exclusion lists determine who should be removed from targeting: recent converters, existing customers, or users in geographic markets the campaign does not serve. These checks confirm that caps are set at the right level and that the exclusion lists used to protect the budget are current.

audit_checks.title: Frequency cap is applied only at the ad group level, not the campaign level
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A user who qualifies for three ad groups in the same campaign can receive the full per-ad-group cap from each one, so actual campaign exposure runs as a multiple of the intended limit. Over-frequency raises cost per result, degrades the user experience, and produces inflated impression counts that obscure true reach.

educate.base:
The Trade Desk's Kokai platform supports frequency caps at the campaign level and at the ad group level. A campaign-level cap limits the total number of impressions a single user receives across every ad group in the campaign, regardless of how many ad groups they qualify for. An ad group-level cap limits impressions from that specific ad group but does not add up across others.

When only ad group-level caps are set, the campaign has no ceiling. A user who qualifies for three ad groups that each cap at five impressions per day can receive up to fifteen impressions from the same campaign in a single day. The platform does not alert when this happens, and the resulting exposure may be far above what the campaign brief intended.

The problem is particularly pronounced in campaigns with audience-based ad groups that overlap. A site visitor segment and a third-party behavioral segment for the same product category may share a large portion of their users. Without a campaign-level cap, those shared users receive impressions from both ad groups simultaneously, and the total becomes a function of how many ad groups they fall into rather than a deliberate creative decision.

A few signs point to this:
  • The campaign has multiple ad groups and no campaign-level frequency cap in the Kokai settings
  • Impression counts are high relative to estimated reach, suggesting users are receiving the ad more often than the intended cap
  • The campaign brief specifies a per-user frequency target that is not reflected in any campaign-level setting
You may want to confirm that a campaign-level cap is in place before reviewing individual ad group caps. The ad group caps only matter within the ceiling the campaign cap sets. The The Trade Desk audience targeting page covers the platform's forecasting tools, which can help estimate reach and frequency before a campaign goes live.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a store, the risk of missing a campaign-level cap is highest during promotional periods when multiple ad groups run simultaneously for the same sale or product launch. A shopper who qualifies for a cart-abandoner ad group, a product-page-visitor ad group, and a prospecting ad group can receive ads from all three at once. You may want to confirm the campaign-level cap reflects the maximum impressions the brand wants to deliver to any one user during the promotion.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, over-frequency produces diminishing returns faster than in retail. A prospect who has already submitted a form, or who has seen the ad many times without converting, should be in a suppression list rather than continuing to receive impressions. Confirming both the campaign-level cap and the suppression list together reduces wasted spend on an audience that has already had sufficient exposure.

educate.learn_more_note: The Trade Desk maintains its documentation for this topic in its partner portal, which requires a logged-in seat and is not publicly accessible. No public reference is linked here. The Trade Desk's free Edge Academy provides public training on its platform, and practitioners with platform access can find the specific guidance in the in-platform Help Center.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether a campaign-level frequency cap is set on each active campaign, and compare it to the ad group-level caps to assess whether the campaign-level setting is the binding constraint.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the Kokai platform and navigate to the campaign you want to audit. Open the campaign settings.
In the campaign settings, locate the frequency cap section. Note whether a campaign-level cap is set and what the impression limit and time window are.
Navigate to each active ad group within the campaign and open the ad group settings. Note the frequency cap at the ad group level for each.

If no campaign-level cap is set but ad group-level caps exist, note the total potential exposures a user who qualifies for multiple ad groups could receive. Multiply the per-ad-group cap by the number of ad groups the user could qualify for simultaneously.
Check the campaign's delivery report for frequency distribution if available. A high average frequency relative to the estimated reach indicates users are receiving the ad more often than intended.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a campaign-level frequency cap is set on each active campaign and what the limit is. A campaign with a campaign-level cap that is equal to or lower than the product of the ad group cap multiplied by the number of simultaneously qualifying ad groups passes. A campaign with no campaign-level cap, or with a campaign-level cap that is higher than the intended per-user maximum from the brief, is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the gap between the ad group-level caps and the campaign-level setting.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the campaign-level and ad group-level frequency caps for each active campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the campaigns on {{site_name}}'s account in The Trade Desk rely on ad group-level frequency caps without a campaign-level cap in place. A user who qualifies for multiple ad groups can receive the full per-ad-group allowance from each one, so the actual impressions delivered to any one user can run well above the intended exposure limit. This raises cost per result and degrades the user experience without surfacing a platform-level alert. {{voice_recommend}} setting a campaign-level frequency cap that reflects the maximum impressions the brief allows per user, and treating the ad group-level caps as a secondary ceiling within that campaign limit rather than the primary control.

audit_checks.title: Suppression and exclusion lists are missing, incomplete, or have not been refreshed
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Spend delivered to recent converters, existing customers, or irrelevant geos is spend that cannot convert at the intended rate. The campaign's cost per acquisition rises, and the reporting does not separate wasted spend from productive spend, so the problem is not visible in standard delivery metrics.

educate.base:
Suppression and exclusion lists in The Trade Desk define audiences that should be removed from targeting. The three most consequential categories are recent converters, existing customers, and geographic markets outside the campaign's intended scope.

A recent-converter suppression list is built from the Universal Pixel's conversion event or from a first-party customer file upload. When a user completes a purchase or a lead form, they move into the suppression segment, and the campaign stops serving to them. Without this list, the campaign continues paying for impressions on users who have already responded. The list only protects spend if it is current: a Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) token-based segment built from a static upload will shrink as tokens rotate unless the upload is refreshed regularly.

An existing-customer exclusion list serves a related but distinct purpose. It removes users who are already customers from prospecting ad groups, so the campaign's acquisition budget does not reach people who have already converted historically, not just in the current flight. This list is typically built from a CRM export and uploaded as a first-party segment.

Geographic exclusions remove locations the campaign is not intended to serve. They are set at the ad group or campaign level as negative geo targets. A campaign running nationally that should exclude certain states or postal codes, or an international campaign that excludes markets where the product is not available, may carry stale or incomplete geo exclusions if the list was never updated after the campaign launched.

A few signs point to gaps in suppression and exclusion:
  • The campaign has no first-party audience segment applied as a negative in the ad group targeting panel
  • The converter suppression segment was last uploaded more than 30 days ago on an account with regular sales volume
  • The geo targeting shows 'All locations' or includes markets the client does not serve
You may want to review all three exclusion types during the same pass. A campaign missing one exclusion type is often missing the others. The UID2 documentation explains how token rotation affects the size and accuracy of first-party segments over time, which is directly relevant to keeping suppression lists current.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a store, the most valuable suppression list is a recent-purchase segment built from the Universal Pixel's purchase event. A user who completed a checkout in the past 14 days rarely needs to see the same acquisition creative again. You may want to confirm this segment is applied as a negative in all prospecting ad groups, that the Universal Pixel is firing the purchase event reliably, and that the segment is refreshed if a CRM file is the source rather than a live pixel feed.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, the post-submission suppression list stops the campaign from serving to users who already submitted a form. This is particularly relevant on campaigns with high form volume, where the submitted-lead pool grows quickly. You may want to confirm the lead data is feeding the suppression segment on a cadence that matches submission volume rather than relying on a one-time upload.

investigate.intro: Confirm that converter suppression, existing-customer exclusion, and geo exclusion lists are applied to active ad groups, and check the recency of each first-party segment used for suppression.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the Kokai platform and navigate to the campaign. For each active ad group, open the targeting panel.
In the audience targeting section, note whether any audience segments are applied as negative targets. Record the segment name and type for each negative audience.
Navigate to the Audiences section and open each first-party segment used for suppression. Note the segment status, the current size, and the last upload or refresh date.

For segments built from a CRM file upload, check whether the last upload predates the most recent sales or lead cycle. A suppression list that does not include the past 30 days of converters is likely missing recent activity.

Return to the ad group targeting panel and confirm that geo targeting does not include markets the campaign is not intended to serve. Note any geographic markets listed that the client does not operate in.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active ad group, whether a converter suppression segment, an existing-customer exclusion segment, and the appropriate geo exclusions are applied as negative targets. An ad group with all three applied, and with first-party suppression segments refreshed within the past 30 days, passes. An ad group missing any of the three, or carrying a suppression segment last uploaded more than 30 days ago, is a fail recorded with the ad group name, the missing or stale exclusion type, and the last upload date where applicable.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the negative audiences and geo exclusions applied to each active ad group, along with the last upload date for any first-party suppression segments, to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more active ad groups on {{site_name}}'s account are missing suppression or exclusion lists, or are relying on first-party suppression segments that have not been refreshed recently enough to reflect current activity. Without a current converter suppression list, the campaign continues serving to users who have already responded. Without an existing-customer exclusion, prospecting spend reaches users who are already customers. Without correct geo exclusions, impressions go to markets the campaign is not intended to serve. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the negative audience and geo targeting on each ad group, uploading or refreshing the first-party suppression segments to reflect the current conversion data, and setting a recurring refresh cadence so the suppression lists stay current for the remainder of the flight.

audit_checks.title: Contradictory targeting layers narrow delivery to the point of preventing spend
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An ad group that combines a narrow audience, a short membership window, a tight frequency cap, and restrictive geo and daypart settings can produce an addressable pool too small to spend the budget. The Kokai platform will not alert to the combination; the ad group simply shows low or zero delivery, which is easy to misread as a bid or inventory problem.

educate.base:
In The Trade Desk, targeting is applied as a set of intersecting filters at the ad group level. The audience segment, geographic target, daypart schedule, device type, inventory environment, and frequency cap all apply simultaneously. Each layer reduces the addressable pool of users the ad group can serve. When several restrictive layers compound, the resulting pool can be too small to absorb the budget, and the ad group delivers well below its pacing target without an obvious explanation in the delivery report.

A few targeting choices account for most delivery gaps from contradictory layers:
  • A narrow first-party segment with a short membership window that keeps the segment small
  • A tight geo target that covers only a small market
  • A frequency cap so low that most eligible users exhaust it early in the day, leaving no eligible impressions for the remainder
  • A restrictive daypart combined with any of the above
Any one of these on its own may be deliberate. The problem arises when they are all applied to the same ad group without checking the combined effect on reach. The Kokai platform's forecasting tool, which shows estimated reach and frequency before the ad group goes live, is the place to catch this before the campaign launches rather than after it has underspent for several days.

The fix is rarely to remove all restrictions. It is to find the layers that are the binding constraint, relax one or two, and confirm in the forecasting tool that the revised setup produces a reachable audience at the intended budget. You may want to evaluate each layer separately before concluding that the ad group needs a more fundamental change.

The The Trade Desk platform's forecasting tools let buyers review audience spend, reach, and frequency estimates before setting the ad group live, which is the most direct way to confirm whether a combination of targeting layers leaves enough addressable inventory.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a store, the combination that produces delivery gaps most frequently in retail is a cart-abandoner segment with a short membership window targeted to a specific city or metro area with a low frequency cap. Cart-abandoner segments are often small by nature. Adding a tight geo and a low cap on top can produce a pool that exhausts itself in the first hour of each day. You may want to run the forecasting tool on any ad group that is underspending despite sufficient campaign budget to confirm whether the targeting combination is the constraint.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, the contradictory-layer problem surfaces most often on campaigns targeting a niche professional audience in a specific geography with a device-type restriction. If the ad group targets a narrow job-title audience segment, limits to a single metro, restricts to desktop, and sets a low frequency cap, the addressable pool may be very small. You may want to review whether the device restriction is necessary. Relaxing it to all devices is often the change that resolves delivery without compromising the audience.

educate.learn_more_note: The Trade Desk maintains its documentation for this topic in its partner portal, which requires a logged-in seat and is not publicly accessible. No public reference is linked here. The Trade Desk's free Edge Academy provides public training on its platform, and practitioners with platform access can find the specific guidance in the in-platform Help Center.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether any active ad group combines targeting layers that together produce an addressable pool too small to serve the budget, and use the platform's forecasting tool to assess the combined effect.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the Kokai platform and identify any ad group that has delivered significantly below its pacing target for more than two days without an obvious budget or bid explanation.
Open the ad group's targeting panel and list every active targeting layer: audience segment and its size, geographic target, daypart schedule, device type, inventory environment, and frequency cap.

Use the forecasting tool within the ad group to estimate the addressable reach given the current combination of targeting layers. Note the estimated daily reach and compare it to the daily impression target implied by the budget and frequency cap.

If the forecasted reach is below what the budget requires to pace correctly, identify which layer or layers are the binding constraint. Try relaxing one layer at a time in the forecasting tool and note which change produces the largest increase in estimated reach.
Record the ad group name, the layers applied, the forecasted reach, and the recommended change.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per underpacing ad group, the combination of targeting layers applied and the forecasted reach from the platform's forecasting tool. An ad group whose forecasted daily reach is sufficient to absorb the budget at the configured frequency cap passes. An ad group whose forecasted reach is too small to spend the budget, traceable to a combination of restrictive targeting layers, is a fail recorded with the ad group name, the binding layers, and the recommended adjustment.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the targeting layers and the forecasting tool output for each underpacing ad group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more ad groups on {{site_name}}'s account in The Trade Desk are combining targeting layers that together narrow the addressable audience to the point where the ad group cannot absorb its budget. The combination of a narrow audience segment, a restrictive geo target, a low frequency cap, or a tight daypart schedule can reduce the reachable pool without triggering a platform alert, so the ad group simply underspends and the gap is easy to attribute to inventory or bid problems rather than targeting configuration. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the targeting layers on each underpacing ad group using the platform's forecasting tool, identifying which layers are the binding constraint, and relaxing one or two to bring the forecasted reach into range with the daily budget target.
